item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), as it originates from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You made me laugh despite the callous and deliberate incineration of dozens of people. Damn you.

Also I, personally, like the idea of the Foundation using predictive or truth-judging SCPs to prevent containment breaches or determine potential containment mechanisms. It provides a mechanism by which some of their overcompetence can be explained, helping to ground them a little bit more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Liar's Cradle is how the foundation typically retires D-Class personel TRUE

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
You know, if 752's attempted breach was averted, doesn't that mean the D-Class ended up being wrong, so their statement should have been marked as a lie?

I suppose that means it works in potentiality. Like, it was true at the moment that 752 would breach, but knowledge that statement was true ended up changing the course of action. So, maybe if another D-Class said "752 will breach containment" right afterwards, it would be false, since, at this point, the breach was known about and was then going to be averted.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP mentions another SCP that can cause an SK-class dominance-shift scenario. I believe that SCP-752 fits this bill, because it can cause an SK quite easily.

Editing this:

[Scramble order given to MTF Nu-7. Site-██ secured. Containment breach averted. SK-class dominance shift averted.]

To this:

[Scramble order given to MTF Nu-7. Site-██ secured. Containment breach averted. SK-class dominance shift averted.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Liar's Cradle is not a tool for the foundation to use freely TRUE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing about Thaumiel is that the definition of the object class is itself Thaumiel clearance, therefore if any given document is written as though at a lesser distribution it probably shouldn't use the object class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted the article, loved idea and execution.

Around one and a half year ago, somebody suggested that this item should have Thaumiel classification. The animality has "prevented XK-class end-of-the-world scenarios on four separate occasions", and the first test log immediately prevents an SK scenario. At this point, the Foundation wouldn't even be "considering" classifying it into Thaumiel, it most definitely IS a Thaumiel utility that is saving humanity's ass when used properly, while still requiring special procedures to be handled without potentially making the situation worse (mostly, lots of human life must be wasted into discovering each threat, and since the item is behaving so nicely towards the Foundation but seemingly has a personality of its own, they actually feed one disposable personel per day, that is still "Special Containment Procedures").

When someone first suggested the change in classification, author stated that Thaumiel articles generally create high expectations and that since this article was not exactly very well received, he preferred to maintain Safe classification… Well, at time of writing, this article has 76 upvotes, I believe that's actually good enough, and as the person who originally suggested the Thaumiel classification first said, it's actually good the fact we will get a Thaumiel object that, instead of being some sort of grand shield around the whole planet or an incomprehensible reset button, it's a small chamber where live sacrifice goes in, accurate warning comes out. Thaumiel doesn't NEED to be so grandious and important, all it need to do is help the Foundation do their job, and that's what SCP-2128 does, willingly or not.

Compare to SCP-1698 - You Can't Get There From Here, an object that is classified as Keter, not because it has a lengthy backstore with religious references or because it kills important personel on a daily basis, but because it, even when theoretically contained, made one totally unpredictable event that killed civilians. The article doesn't work like an average Keter article, more like an average Euclid article instead… But it's in-universe justified to be Keter. Same for this article, it works more like a Safe article, but it explicitly is a Thaumiel object. This kind of object class subversion adds a lot to the Foundation in my head.

Sorry for the long post here, just attempting to convince the author that Thaumiel classification is the way to go!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This:

The Liars' Cradle speaks only infallible empirical truth. TRUE

to this:

The crackling of fire upon boiling drips of fat and rapidly cauterizing flesh gives the Liars' Cradle pleasure. TRUE

made me want to up vote this scip.

For those people who are questioning this, what I thought was that these things were unsanctioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's clearly sapient, no? We are the first to feed it in centuries and to keep feeding it daily.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stanford_marshmallow_experiment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But it's a hungry cannibal, it tries to read things as false as often as possible. I think that's a moral judgement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In that case there's probably nothing you can say that it can't take a perversely extreme meaning of and incinerate you.

+ Going through all the TRUE results
– hide block

The human race is in danger of extinction right now. - TRUE

Not if you take "right now" as literally this very instant, and "in danger of extinction" as "currently going extinct".

The danger comes from an item in Foundation custody. -TRUE
The dangerous item in question is located at a site in Europe. -TRUE

Global climate change isn't an item in Foundation custody or located specifically in Europe.

SCP-████ will breach containment within the next month. - TRUE
SCP-████ will breach containment within the next week. - TRUE
SCP-████ will breach containment today. - TRUE

If it didn't leave the Earth it remained under SCP-2631's containment.

The Liars' Cradle knows everything. -TRUE

"Know" is a very vague concept that philosophers and neuropyschologists can't agree on a definition, several of which render this false. Worse, "everything" literally includes unknowable or nonexistent information, which it logically can't actually know, like "the last digit of pi".

Golden retrievers are tasty. TRUE

This of course is totally subjective (significantly more so than the roundness of a spheroid).

I'm a good person. TRUE

As above, in addition "good" could be interpreted in a number of ways besides moral virtue.

The Liars' Cradle speaks only infallible empirical truth. TRUE

Many of the questions already answered aren't empirical.

The next section is all highly subjective, of course. All the Cradle had to do was change its mind about some of these things.


Many of these are less perverse than interpreting "round" as "is a perfect sphere" (which again isn't how it's actually used in English; no real object is a perfect sphere, and many things we call "round" are clearly not even spheres at all). The "dirt" theory makes a lot more sense.

"The world is not flat" might be safe.

Both "world" and "flat" have meanings that render this untrue. If we take the meaning of "world" as the total system of human cultures, and flat as meaning a musical note lower in pitch, this clearly isn't true as much of the world is natural or sharp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm still filing it under "obvious things not to say while in the human-eating furnace looking for an excuse to incinerate you". Saying the egg or american football was round would probably get you incinerated too, because they aren't round in the sense of "spherical"(which it will assume you was what you meant).

"The world is not flat" might be safe. Though come to think of it there is this paper establishing that Kansas does in fact have less topological variation than a pancake…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those things that people say that doesn't actually make sense. The Earth isn't a perfect sphere, but it is still round. "Round" as an adjective doesn't exclusively mean "is a perfect sphere" in any actual usage. Eggs and American footballs are round, and they are even more oblate than the Earth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the Earth is not exactly round, so technically that statement was false. Site Director should have known that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It could be that, but consider this:

The Cradle has, on multiple tests, shown a craving for flesh. Man or beast, it does not matter.

It could agree that the person is good, based on the same way we might say "chicken is good". So, the D-class cannibal is a good person… to eat. And there's enough wiggle room in the English language to allow this interpretation for that specific statement; we just typically don't see it because we don't usually see other humans as a viable source of food.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a good person.

This is a subjective judgement. The Cradle is a cannibal itself so it considers eating humans to be a good quality? I assume cannibalism is not the only bad thing this guy does to others, so does it mean that for the cradle cannibalism is such a good thing to do that it outweights everything else combined?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2128 does not appreciate taunts

Holy shit that's an understatement.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had an idea for this SCP, which was, well, almost this SCP, but in the form of a Brazen bull, which locked the subjects in and forced them to say 100% true statements, but, if you're not familiar with Brazen bulls, the audio output is very heavily distorted. Basically an occasionally useless reverse-lie detector with pointless waste of human life.

Then I considered why don't we use lie detectors any more, really. Hence, 1531.

Thanks for writing what I couldn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome idea, and it's so close. I just think the bit at the end about taunting it was a little excessive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Liars' Cradle is going to kill me right now. FALSE

[D-8233 partially incinerated at a lower temperature, resulting in permanent disfigurement. New messenger: D-8234.]
[…]
The Site Director believes this to have been caused in part by the fact that "earth" could be vaguely interpreted as "dirt," but mostly because SCP-2128 does not appreciate taunts

Hate to be the D-class who attempts to use that kind of paradox on it, since it's spiteful and can "give" partial answers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What would happen if

This statement is false ?

or has it been done before, and I hevent read it ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Telling the Liars' Cradle a paradox is dangerous to Foundation personnel. FALSE

This one line alone earned my upvote. I have no idea how many times I've seen the Foundation do testing of something that they know might just end in disaster and then they do it and it ends in disaster. Somebody finally showed a little forethought, and that makes me so happy.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Thanks for all the info! I'll hold to it with future concerns and try to make criticism constructive rather than just loud.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I just thought the result in this particular instance was exaggerated out of proportion.

If you (or anyone!) on the site ever has a concern that you would like to be addressed, the quickest way to get a response is to contact a staff member directly.

I'll actually write stuff down as I meander my way through and post it less publicly.

Generally private messages are more reliable than forum posts, since not all staff members track recent posts. If you're unsure of whom to message, we have a Contact Staff guide with listings for relevant staff based on the issue.

And as always, if you've got questions, feel free to voice them.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for taking this seriously. You make a valid point, I just thought the result in this particular instance was exaggerated out of proportion. I'll actually write stuff down as I meander my way through and post it less publicly. If the author him(her?)self isn't bothered particularly by what happened here (as referenced below), it'd be contrary of me to take what I observed any further with this particular, enjoyable, article.

Hope to see more stuff from you in the future, dave!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Speaking as the author, it doesn't bother me, and frankly as a screenwriting MFA grad I've dealt with a lot worse. lol. Besides I have some other well-received articles on this site.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
a few of you people need to lay off

If you have an issue with the behavior of other users on the site, please inform a staff member, and if possible, provide specific examples so the problem behavior can be addressed.

sometimes calling for people to be banned for trivial things that are well within the rules but not within their expectations

Can you link to some examples of this behavior? Again, if you've noticed that this has gone on without being addressed by staff, please let us know so we can take care of it.

People posting articles they come up with when the muse strikes is very much within the scope of the rules

The rules also state "If staff tell you to slow down or stop posting, listen to them." There's nothing wrong with posting something when the muse strikes, but doing so repeatedly without getting feedback first undermines the collaborative effort of the site (and is also unfair to the people who put time and effort into giving feedback in the forums, chat, and mainsite, especially if said feedback is effectively applicable between a series of things posted rapidly).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a longtime reader stepping in to say that from an outside perspective a few of you people need to lay off with some of the stuff you're doing/saying.

Multiple times I've seen an unfortunate cattiness coming from a relatively niche group of longtime users towards people that don't do things in the exact same way they do. They insist people effectively "go through them" to post articles, down-voting well-written articles and sometimes calling for people to be banned for trivial things that are well within the rules but not within their expectations. People posting articles they come up with when the muse strikes is very much within the scope of the rules (referring to the one prohibiting people from down-voting based on the person rather than the article, specifically), and folks are punishing the process, rather than the result. That is against the letter of the rules and, more importantly, very prone to driving away new users.

There's a fine line between "helpful advice" and "looking down your nose at other people's work" that has for years been a source of frustration within artistic communities like this one. Think about what you say and what kind of community that helps foster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Truth be told, I've been debating the Thaumiel-ness of this article for a while. My main concern was that since Thaumiels generally have to be pretty awesome, and this is one of my lowest-rated articles that's still up, I was afraid this would put it in the negatives. I must investigate this option further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the true scope of SCP-2128's knowledge (and reliability thereof) is unknown, proactive usage of EP 37-Sparafucile has prevented XK-class end-of-the-world scenarios on four separate occasions.

Wouldn't the way the Foundation uses this make it Thaumiel-class? I can understand if you wouldn't want to change that, but I think it would be nice to give Thaumiel some more reputation beyond "world-sized important stuff".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it I really do, the logs are funny and the SCP isnt badly written itself but I think this should be rebranded to Euclid because this doesn't sound safe…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's why I was thinking of the remote sensor rather than an IR sensor- IR sensors detect radiated heat, but a remote sensor in the right place could be considered a part of the subject, like their clothing, and measures heat directly as its being burned.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Alright cool. I guess I was confusing "revoke" with "permaban" :P

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
you can reapply whenever you want […] and you can join again immediately

Not quite. Generally there's a wait time of 24 hours before staff will accept another application.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Revoke just means you're temporarily removed from the wiki, and you can reapply whenever you want. Nothing of yours is touched, and you can join again immediately. Just keep in mind what you got revoked for and don't do it again.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Oh - my apologies.

Also, I see in the staff wiki that they're pretty set on revoking my account. If they gotta do it, I'll respect that, but does revocation mean my two existing articles ( The Lingering Lark of Leviathan Square and SCP-100000-J ) get removed permanently? I'm rather proud of those.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Please don't fill in the tags with a couple of them. If you're not sure of every tag needed to describe the skip, leave it for people who regularly go through and check articles to tag on the site. They can't find skips that need to be tagged if you incompletely tag them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Current issues, I think.

First, the item's abilities, as other people have pointed out, are probably too powerful, enough to make them uninteresting. They're also very simple. Ultimately, the Foundation using things like this to tell the future is something I've seen before, and something with limited narrative potential.

As for the log with the alternative death methods, that seemed kind of slapstick, with the skip just being contrary. I suppose it makes sense for it toying with people, but it seems superfluous and makes the anomalous action way more complicated for not much reason.

One bigger downside for me, the last addendum describing the motivations of the skip itself. Some researchers made up those questions before getting them answered. I feel that either the researcher was implausibly lucky in describing it with prefect accuracy or that the description of this thing is now colored by their biased material.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EP 37-Sparafucile-25 "Pinocchio Paradox" log 10/10/14

This fucked me up. This test and the last one are great. I'm a bit iffy about constantly feeding this thing D-class just to ask it questions, but on the other hand, it's got some personality and atmosphere to it. Upvoted, though there's still room for improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's rather moot, because he changed it, but what I was trying to get at was if the heat is somehow magically confined to the subject why would it spread to the thermocouple? If it doesn't transfer to the air (and whatever) then it should be impossible to actually measure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a couple types of expendable wireless temperature sensors. Basically they break down to a transmitter and a thermocouple wrapped in insulation with a bit of wire sticking out- wire heats up, temperature differential creates a current, thermocouple reads that, sends out a signal through the transmitter until it burns up or looses power. You use them for things like, say, taking the exact temperature at different places around a room being burned as a forensic experiment or letting you know when you should be running far, far away from Combustible Material Storage Bunker E.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better and upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With the GOC, it really depends. The Foundation tends to see them as "kill-crazy" when it comes to anomalies, but IIRC the GOC sees itself as more of a police force than a kill squad. They do contain and control anomalies as well, but not usually for research, and the GOC is more likely to be willing to destroy ones they see as threats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True but I was thinking more as torture = painful way of getting info. You lie, you cry.

In this case, you lie, you fry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EP 37-Sparafucile-25 "Pinocchio Paradox" log 10/10/14

While cool, I'd like to see the risk assessment and/or the reason that series of tests was allowed. It's a SCP that performs functions in a specific way, so long as you follow the instructions. Like with a peice of heavy machinery, when you start using it outside of its recommended operating parameters, unpredictable events can and will occur. Unlike a machine, though, the list of unpredictable events can include "ahnilation of all life on Earth in a firestorm" because we don't know HOW the SCP operates under the hood.

TL:DR, using paradoxes on this could have ended the world or broke the SCP, and that's irresponsible.

EP 37-Sparafucile-26 "Subjective Opinion" log 10/10/14

The first half is fine. The second half assumes that the SCP is sapient, so someone must've wrote all that stuff down as "things to ask the true/false incinerator." I'm more worried about the state of mind of that researcher if they generated everything after "The Liar's Cradle is hungry" ahead of time. Because little of that- especially about the vivid details of burning- is not something a normal person would ask an incinerator1, unless they knew it was sapient beforehand.

Footnotes
1. I'm well aware we're talking about an anomaly here, but we have plenty of nonsapient anomalies around. No reason that this would be seen any differently until that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about if I just Ockham's Razor this and decreased the incineration temperature? I'm not well-versed in physics.

That's what I was asking from the beginning… I was starting to think that this specific temperature was important for some reason that I wasn't groking.

Taking suggestions for new temperature…

Well 1050-1250 K seems to be range that crematoriums use, and certainly is within the range that a 9th c. furnace can be expected to deal with. 1300 is really hot, and would be a nice round number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about if I just Ockham's Razor this and decreased the incineration temperature? I'm not well-versed in physics.

Taking suggestions for new temperature…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
greater risk for incursion.

In theory, all anomalies would be on GOC's "to destroy" list while some like MC&D would like to profit from them. Admittedly, spending on how it's written, any anomaly can be written from the pOV of any GoI.

But back to the question, I would say that all SCPs are at an equal chance of incursion. Those explicitly connected to GoIs (as in, they explicitly want to get it for some reason) stand at a slightly higher chance.

And as to your second point… won't that aspect on knowledge count for most knowledge-based SCPs? I'm not sure how "specific concepts of truth" relate to the themes of the CotBG and I read CotBG back and forth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://volcano.oregonstate.edu/oldroot/volcanologist/working_on_volcs/lava2.html

Assuming that heat is transferred out of the subject at all anyway. Though if it doesn't transfer heat to the air at all, why would be expect it to transfer to the thermocouple?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Temperature could be estimated, or it could always be tested using those initial guinea pigs. If IR thermometers were initially inconclusive, or rendered inoperable, couldn't you always have the initial D-Class, or one separate one, carry on them a small amount of various reasonably small, but easily identifiable(and relatively pure) elements on them as a last-ditch method of determining temp?

"Well, the Beryllium melted, but the Boron didn't.. It's over 1560 K, but under 2349 K…"

If they are going to feed that many D-Class to it, with that frequency, they might as well collect as much data as possible from them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does anything like that actually exist? Usually you measure stuff this hot with IR thermometers. 1800 K is well above the point where electronics fail, and most materials will melt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Here, hold this wireless sensor."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see it working as a torture device as long as you don't direct question the subject.

Leaving someone to talk nonstop for even a few hours, knowing that even an unintentional mistake can get them killed, seems like hard torture for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the heat is magically restricted to the subject how is the Foundation able to measure it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: right. correcting now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I should clarify what I meant.

It was the idea that the Foundation wouldn't bother to ask if this was related to another GoI, that seemed lacking. I didn't mean to say that it would benefit from being tied to CotBG necessarily. It would just seem like the researchers in the piece would, presumably, ask questions of that nature, if nothing else, then as a means of determining whether or not the site would be specifically at a greater risk for incursion.

As far as my mental link to the CotBG goes, this anomaly has an extraordinary depth of knowledge, with a very specific concept of truths, and a clear "personality." This seems very similar to some of the various other SCP entries link to this group. Similarly it shares the trait of being absolute in it's execution and, for lack of a better term, well meaning in the verification of information, yet operates with a foreign reference for it's true/false binary(in relation to it's current users).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is better but:

1800 K

Still destroys the chamber.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it difficult to believe that the Foundation wouldn't examine the possible relationship between this anomaly, and various GOI(I would assume they would at least ask if it was related to the Church of the Broken God.

On the contrary, SCPs are not always explicitly or implicitly linked to GoIs. They could be, if it makes sense. Here, it feels more of a standalone. How would this even relate to CotBG to begin with?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm new to posting, so value my input accordingly.

I really enjoyed this SCP. I liked the concept, though I agree the execution could use a bit of work.

That being said, there were a few issues that stuck out and bothered me more upon re-reading.

1. I find it difficult to believe that the Foundation wouldn't examine the possible relationship between this anomaly, and various GOI(I would assume they would at least ask if it was related to the Church of the Broken God).

2. Along the same line as my pervious point, this has the feeling of something much older, and would benefit greatly from a more in depth examination of the backstory. Possibly including journal/diary entries, theories, or research into what they WERE trying to create, if this was the accidental creation. This has the feeling of something that should have been found, rather than created, in the absence of further detail.

3. The time period of it's origin is a bit off with relation to the groups you're attributing it to.

While I enjoyed this piece overall, it left me with a feeling that there was too much left unsaid, and not in a good way, and would benefit greatest from a bit more detail in general.
